content.text:
<user_query>
This is more of a design question. I want to transition Kafka customers to apache iggy. I am changing iggy code base to accomidate the needful changes. I will come to that in later prompts. In this prompt I want to understand all about Kafka API Keys, its various versions. Please provide a comprehensove information such that I can design my code base in Iggy to support the Kafka existing customers
</user_query>

Great direction. If you want Kafka clients to “just work” against Iggy, understanding **Kafka API keys + version negotiation** is the core foundation.

## Mental Model: Kafka Wire Protocol

Every Kafka request on the wire is:

- `RequestHeader` + `RequestBody`
- Header includes:
  - `api_key` (which API)
  - `api_version` (schema version for that API)
  - `correlation_id`
  - `client_id`

Each API is identified by a numeric **API key** (for example `Produce`, `Fetch`, `Metadata`, etc.), and each API has many **versions** (`v0`, `v1`, …).

### Why versions matter
- Kafka evolves by adding/removing/reshaping fields.
- Same logical operation (`Fetch`) can look different in v2 vs v12.
- Clients choose versions based on broker support.

---

## How Clients Discover Supported Versions

### `ApiVersions` handshake (critical)
Most clients first call `ApiVersions` (API key 18) to ask:
- which API keys broker supports
- min/max version per key

Client then picks the highest mutually supported version.

### Design implication for Iggy
Implement `ApiVersions` early and make it accurate.  
This lets clients gracefully downgrade instead of failing hard.

---

## API Key Families You Need to Know

For migration compatibility, think by **client persona**:

## 1) Producer path (must-have first)

- `Produce` (0)
- `Metadata` (3)
- `FindCoordinator` (10) for idempotent/transactional paths and some client flows
- `InitProducerId` (22) for idempotence/transactions
- Transaction APIs if you want transactional producers:
  - `AddPartitionsToTxn` (24)
  - `AddOffsetsToTxn` (25)
  - `EndTxn` (26)
  - `TxnOffsetCommit` (28)
  - `WriteTxnMarkers` (27) (broker/internal path)
- `ApiVersions` (18)

If you only support non-transactional initially, you can still support many producers by:
- implementing non-idempotent `Produce`
- clearly advertising unsupported transaction APIs via `ApiVersions`

---

## 2) Consumer path (must-have first)

- `Fetch` (1)
- `ListOffsets` (2)
- `OffsetCommit` (8)
- `OffsetFetch` (9)
- Group management APIs:
  - `FindCoordinator` (10)
  - `JoinGroup` (11)
  - `Heartbeat` (12)
  - `LeaveGroup` (13)
  - `SyncGroup` (14)
  - `DescribeGroups` (15), `ListGroups` (16) for admin tooling
- `Metadata` (3)
- `ApiVersions` (18)

Without group APIs, only manual assignment consumers will work reliably.

---

## 3) Admin / ecosystem compatibility

Commonly used by Kafka AdminClient / tooling:

- `CreateTopics` (19), `DeleteTopics` (20)
- `DescribeConfigs` (32), `AlterConfigs` (33), incremental alter configs (44)
- `CreatePartitions` (37)
- ACL APIs (`CreateAcls`/`DeleteAcls`/`DescribeAcls`) if security model needs parity
- `DescribeCluster` (60), `DescribeProducers` (61), `DescribeTransactions` (65) in newer ecosystems
- `DeleteRecords` (21)
- `ElectLeaders` (43) (optional if no leader election semantics)

You can postpone many of these if your initial target is app runtime (producer/consumer), not admin parity.

---

## Versioning Concepts That Affect Implementation

## A) Per-API version ranges
Each API has independent version evolution.  
Example: `Produce v3` and `Fetch v12` are unrelated version lines.

## B) “Flexible versions” (KIP-482)
Newer versions encode fields compactly and allow **tagged fields**.
This changes serialization format significantly.

Design choice:
- start with classic (non-flexible) versions for core APIs
- then add flexible versions once baseline compatibility is stable

## C) Record/message formats
`Produce`/`Fetch` carry record batches (message format evolution: magic v0/v1/v2).  
Modern clients generally use record batch format from newer Kafka.

## D) Error codes and throttle times
Clients are sensitive to Kafka error semantics:
- return correct Kafka error codes
- include `throttle_time_ms` where required by version schema

## E) Nullable/default fields across versions
A lot of compatibility bugs come from:
- wrong nullability
- missing default values
- wrong array encoding for a version

---

## Authentication / Security APIs (if needed)

Depending on your deployment goals:

- `SaslHandshake` (17)
- `SaslAuthenticate` (36)
- TLS (outside Kafka API keys but expected by many deployments)

If you don’t support these initially, document clearly and ensure clients configured for PLAINTEXT can connect.

---

## Practical Compatibility Strategy for Iggy

## Phase 1: “Minimum usable Kafka facade”
Implement well:
- `ApiVersions`
- `Metadata`
- `Produce` (at least one widely used version)
- `Fetch`
- `ListOffsets`

This enables basic produce/consume for many simple clients.

## Phase 2: Consumer group parity
Add stable support for:
- `FindCoordinator`, `JoinGroup`, `SyncGroup`, `Heartbeat`, `LeaveGroup`
- `OffsetCommit`, `OffsetFetch`

This unlocks mainstream consumer group behavior.

## Phase 3: Producer guarantees
Add idempotence/transactions:
- `InitProducerId` and txn APIs
- exactly-once semantics mapping (if possible in Iggy architecture)

## Phase 4: Admin ecosystem
Add topic/config/admin APIs based on customer tooling needs.

---

## How to Choose Which Versions to Support First

Pick by **real customer client libraries** and versions:

- Java clients (`org.apache.kafka:kafka-clients`) version ranges
- librdkafka-based clients (Confluent .NET/Go/C/C++)
- Python (`confluent-kafka`, `kafka-python`)
- Node (`kafkajs`, `node-rdkafka`)

Then:
1. Observe which API versions they request in handshake.
2. Support those exact version bands first.
3. Expand outward.

This is better than trying to implement “all latest versions” blindly.

---

## Architecture Recommendation for Iggy Kafka Layer

Build protocol handling as:

- **API key router**: dispatch by `(api_key, api_version)`
- **Versioned schema codecs**: per API/version decode+encode modules
- **Semantic core layer**: translate Kafka request semantics to Iggy internal operations
- **Error mapper**: internal errors -> Kafka error codes
- **Capabilities registry**: single source used by `ApiVersions`

This keeps protocol churn isolated from Iggy core logic.

---

## Common Migration Pitfalls

- Incorrect `ApiVersions` (clients mis-negotiate and fail mysteriously)
- Supporting `Produce` but not compatible `Metadata` details
- Partial group protocol behavior causing rebalance storms
- Returning success where Kafka expects retriable/fatal specific errors
- Ignoring flexible-version encoding differences
- Inconsistent topic/partition metadata semantics

---

If you want, next prompt I can give you a **concrete implementation matrix** like:

- “If you need to support Java client X + librdkafka Y, implement these exact APIs/versions first”
- plus a **test harness plan** (golden protocol tests + compatibility tests against real clients).

<user_query>
Does cursor save these responses or should I save outside of cursor? 
</user_query>

Cursor does keep chat history, so your responses are generally saved in the conversation automatically.

For important design docs (like this Kafka/Iggy migration plan), still keep a copy outside chat (for example in your repo docs or Notion), because that makes it easier to version, review, and share with your team.

If you want, I can turn the Kafka API/version guidance into a clean `docs/` markdown file structure you can keep in your codebase.

<user_query>
Please go ahead and do the needful, let the repo name be iggy_supporting_kafka
</user_query>

I’ll create a new local repo folder named `iggy_supporting_kafka` and add a structured markdown knowledge pack there so you have this saved outside chat. Next I’ll include a concise README plus a detailed Kafka API keys/versions design guide.
(continued in repo docs creation and updates)
